title: Spinning Bluefaced Leicester
subtitle: How BFL fleece's long staple and high lustre affect drafting, twist, and finished yarn character
excerpt: Bluefaced Leicester is a long-staple, lustrous British longwool with a staple length of 10-15 cm and a micron count around 24-28. It requires a different approach from fine merino: a longer draw, more twist, and patience with the slippery fibres at the start of the draft. Finished BFL yarn has a characteristic high sheen and a smooth, cool hand.
sourceNotes: BFL spinning technique is documented in UK spinning guild instruction and longwool fibre preparation literature. Method draws on observation-led instruction for long-staple breeds.

attrs.body: Keep hair and loose clothing back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin spindle while the wheel is running.
attrs.title: Spinning wheel safety

content.text: and the worsted draw to make the most of its lustre. Fine-wool spinning habits (carding into rolags, woollen short draw) produce a thick, somewhat fluffy BFL yarn that loses the characteristic sheen. Most commercial BFL top is already combed and ready for the worsted draw.

items.name: Spinning wheel (Saxony or castle) or top-whorl drop spindle
Yarn swift and ball winder for winding off

content.text: Pull off a length of BFL top about 30 cm long and split it lengthways into a strip about 2-3 cm wide. BFL top is dense; splitting it into narrower preparations makes it easier to draft evenly. Drafting the full-width top produces very thick singles.
Step 2: Start with the worsted draw

In the worsted draw, the front hand (closer to the orifice) pinches the drafted yarn and the back hand drafts back from behind the front hand. Twist is never allowed to enter the drafting zone. For BFL, move the back hand further than for merino: the long staple needs a longer draft zone (10-15 cm rather than 5-8 cm for short-staple wools). Aim for a consistent, smooth motion.

BFL needs more twist than merino to hold together, because the long fibres do not lock around each other as readily as shorter fibres. Use a twist-per-inch (TPI) that is 15-20% higher than you would for a comparable merino singles. On a wheel, this means using a faster whorl or increasing treadle speed slightly. The finished singles should feel springy when stretched along their length, not limp or ropey.
Step 4: Ply, set, and assess the sheen

Ply two BFL singles into a 2-ply using S-twist (opposite to the Z-twist of the singles). Wind off onto a niddy-noddy, tie the skein loosely in four places, and set the twist by soaking in lukewarm water for 20 minutes. Snap the skein between your hands three or four times while still wet to settle the plying twist. Hang to dry under a small weight. The finished skein should show a clear, smooth lustre along its length.

items.fix: Split the top to half its commercial width before spinning. Extend the back-hand draft further than you are used to from short-staple spinning.
items.cause: BFL top not split into narrow enough preparations, or drafting zone too short for the long staple
items.problem: The yarn is thick-thin rather than consistent

items.fix: Use the worsted draw throughout: never allow twist into the drafting zone. Carding BFL destroys the fibre alignment that produces the sheen.
items.cause: Woollen preparation or draw rather than worsted draw
items.problem: The finished yarn lacks lustre despite using BFL

items.fix: Increase twist by reducing treadle or tying off more slowly. BFL needs noticeably more twist than merino of the same weight.
items.problem: The yarn is limp and separates easily when the skein is snapped
